sections.title: Meta
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Market | Facilities Support Services |
| NAICS | 561210 |
| VN file | VN_facilities_support_services.md |
| Türwächter anchor | L4 Door Guardian Device → L5 Door & Exit Monitoring → L6e Safety & Compliance Management |
| Architecture distance | 2 |
| Offering level | L7 (service ecosystem) |
| Routing | B2B / service NAICS — Phase 1 skips BOM (1.4) and Edges (1.5) |

---

sections.title: Derivation Chain
sections.content:
```
CFJ: "Operate and maintain a client's built environment to achieve continuous safety
     compliance, operational readiness, and regulatory conformance while minimising
     client-side labour"
  extracted from NAICS 561210 description
  |
  output types → IFM, HS, SS, CFM, PFM (5 service engagement types)
  |
  Process VN → L6e Safety & Regulatory Compliance Management (primary insertion point)
               L6c Planned Preventive Maintenance (battery/device maintenance cycle)
  |
  company enters → L4 Door Guardian Device within L5 Door & Exit Monitoring System
  |
  parent unit → L5 Door & Exit Monitoring System (defines job context)
  |
  lens → "What detection and documentation capabilities does an FM safety compliance
          manager need to deliver continuous, evidence-grade monitoring of emergency
          exit doors across client building portfolios?"
  |
  Product Job → "Help Door & Exit Monitoring System to detect unauthorized emergency
                 exit door activations and produce audit-ready event logs for FM
                 compliance workflows — without wired infrastructure or manual
                 inspection rounds"
  |
  alternatives → Hardwired electronic (45%), Battery-powered wireless (35%),
                 Mechanical-only (20%), CCTV monitoring, Do nothing
  |
  scenarios → New client onboarding, Annual fire audit, Incident investigation,
              Battery maintenance cycle
  |
  pyramid (Executor) → P1 per alternative (5 lifecycle stages)
                     → P2 core job (7 error types × 8 job map steps)
                     → P3 Role / P4 Image / P5 Emotional
  |
  market needs → 22 error statements (ODI scored)
                 Top underserved: N04 (16), N08 (16), N14 (16)
```

---

sections.title: Phase 1 — Industry Landscape
sections.content:
### 1.1 CFJ, Output Types, Offering Level

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| NAICS | 561210 — Facilities Support Services |
| CFJ | Operate and maintain a client's built environment to achieve continuous safety compliance, operational readiness, and regulatory conformance while minimising client-side labour |
| Offering Level | L7 — service ecosystem: FM provider coordinates sub-contractors, vendors, and regulators toward operational readiness of client buildings |

| Code | Output Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| IFM | Integrated FM | Full multi-discipline bundled contract (hard + soft + compliance) |
| HS | Hard Services | MEP, fire safety, structural maintenance only |
| SS | Soft Services | Cleaning, security, catering, landscaping |
| CFM | Compliance FM | Regulatory inspection and documentation only |
| PFM | Portfolio FM | Multi-site, multi-building management |

L6e (Safety & Compliance Management) is the **only** core process step activated by CFM — the narrow wedge where the Türwächter anchors. L6a and L6f activate all 5 output types but are commodity steps with low competitive moat.

### 1.2 Segments

Defined by FM operator circumstance, not organisation size or sector.

| Segment | Circumstance | Alternatives change? |
|---|---|---|
| Portfolio FM operator | 20+ client sites across mixed building types; requires standardised devices deployable per-site and centralised multi-building dashboard | Yes — single-site solutions don't aggregate; wired solutions don't scale cost-effectively |
| Compliance FM specialist | Inspection-only contracts; client demands digital, audit-proof evidence chains with no paper archives or manual log assembly | Yes — documentation capability becomes the competitive differentiator |
| Single-site IFM contract | One large building (hospital campus, corporate HQ); deep BMS integration required; wired alternatives reduce integration friction | Yes — proprietary gateway friction (C6) tips decision toward wired for BMS-heavy sites |
| Hard Services operator | MEP and fire safety maintenance only; device must be low-maintenance and high-reliability across diverse door hardware types | Yes — maintenance overhead per device is the primary total cost driver |

Anti-pattern avoided: "Large FM companies vs. small FM companies" is demographics. "Portfolio management vs. inspection-only contracts" is circumstance.

### 1.3 Process VN

Full VN documented in **VN_facilities_support_services.md** (L7→L6→L5→L4, ~45 nodes).

Key insertion path:
- L6e Safety & Regulatory Compliance Management → L5 Door & Exit Monitoring System → **L4 Door Guardian Device** (anchor)
- Adjacent complement: L5 Fire Safety Inspection Platform feeds L6f compliance documentation

### 1.4–1.5 BOM / Edges

**Skipped.** NAICS 561210 is a service sector code. Service composition is documented in the VN file. No physical product BOM applies.

---

sections.title: Phase 2 — Company Enters
sections.content:
### 2.1 VN Anchor

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Anchor type | PROCESS_VN |
| Anchor unit | Door Guardian Device |
| Anchor level | L4 |
| Parent unit (job context) | Door & Exit Monitoring System (L5) |
| Grand-parent | Safety & Regulatory Compliance Management (L6e) |
| Route | B2B — FM operator is buyer; building owner is beneficiary |

The Türwächter does not end up *in* the FM service output. It enables the FM operator to *perform* compliance management. PROCESS_VN confirmed. The parent L5 unit (Door & Exit Monitoring System) defines the job context for all downstream need derivation.

### 2.2 Lens

| Route | Lens Source | Lens |
|---|---|---|
| Maker — PROCESS_VN | L6e job_family: "Inspect, test, document, and certify compliance with fire safety, health, and environmental regulations across client sites" | "What detection and documentation capabilities does an FM safety compliance manager need to deliver continuous, evidence-grade monitoring of emergency exit doors across client building portfolios?" |

All 4 segments pass Level A filter (L6e serves IFM/HS/CFM/PFM) and Level B filter (product modality applies across commercial/institutional door hardware in all FM contract types).

### 2.3 Product Job

| Job Level | Statement |
|---|---|
| CFJ (L7) | Operate and maintain a client's built environment to achieve continuous safety compliance, operational readiness, and regulatory conformance while minimising client-side labour |
| **Product Job (L4 anchored to L5 parent)** | **Help Door & Exit Monitoring System to detect unauthorized emergency exit door activations and produce audit-ready event logs for FM compliance workflows — without requiring wired infrastructure or on-site inspection rounds** |

Abstraction test passed: 5 alternatives from distinct technology categories can fulfill this product job (see 2.4). Correctly a Product Job, not a scenario.

### 2.4 Alternatives

| # | Alternative | UNSPSC | Inherent Trade-offs |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | Hardwired electronic alarm systems (door contacts wired to fire panel) | 46171600 | Requires mains cabling and conduit in retrofit buildings; electrician sign-off; €150–300 labour/door on top of hardware; single cable-fault can drop a circuit; asbestos risk in pre-1980 buildings |
| **B** | **Battery-powered wireless alarm devices (Türwächter class, LoRaWAN door sensors)** | **46171600** | **Battery replacement cycles (1–3 yr); wireless range limits (~30–60 m); proprietary protocol friction (EasyWave ≠ BACnet/KNX); higher unit cost than mechanical** |
| C | Mechanical-only door alarms (no monitoring, no wireless) | 46171501 | No event logging; no remote alerting; compliance evidence requires manual inspection; declining installed base; no portfolio visibility |
| D | CCTV / video monitoring at exit points | 46171610 | No alarm-on-open; requires active human review; high infrastructure cost; GDPR data-retention complexity; no audit-grade timestamped event log |
| E | Do nothing / manual inspection rounds [Status Quo] | — | **Zero P1 burden** — FM inspection labor cost only (est. €6–12/door/visit quarterly); compliance gap risk accepted |

**Existential flag:** Alternative E (do nothing) carries zero P1 burden and remains the dominant incumbent at lower-spend or lower-scrutiny FM contracts. It competes on cost alone.

### 2.5 Scenarios

| Scenario | Output Types Activated | L4 Positions in Play |
|---|---|---|
| New client onboarding — FM deploys exit monitoring across 50+ doors | IFM, HS, PFM | Door Guardian Device, Wireless Radio Transmitter, Gateway / BUS Interface |
| Annual fire safety audit — FM generates compliance evidence for fire marshal | IFM, CFM, PFM | Door Guardian Device (event log), Fire Safety Inspection Platform |
| Unauthorized exit incident — FM retrieves historical event logs for client incident report | IFM, HS, CFM, PFM | Door Guardian Device (log), Gateway / BUS Interface |
| Battery maintenance cycle — FM schedules battery replacement across all monitored doors | IFM, HS, PFM | Battery Power Unit, CMMS work order (L5) |

Sparsity check: SS (soft services) never activates any of these scenarios — correctly dormant.

---

sections.title: Phase 3 — Needs Derivation
sections.content:
### Stakeholder Roles

| Role | Person | Pyramid Levels |
|---|---|---|
| **Job Executor** | FM safety compliance engineer / compliance technician | ALL (P1–P5) — primary needs source |
| **Job Overseer** | FM contract manager / safety director | P1 (own), P2 (own), P4, P5 (rarely) |
| **Job Beneficiary** | Building owner / client operations director | P2, P3, P5 |
| Lifecycle Support — Installer | FM field technician | P1 Preparation only |
| Lifecycle Support — Maintainer | FM maintenance engineer | P1 Maintenance only |
| Purchase Executor | FM procurement manager | P1 Acquisition only |
| Purchase Influencer | Fire safety consultant / building insurer | P2, P4 |

### 3.1 Burleson P1 — Product Jobs by Alternative

| Lifecycle Stage | Alt A: Hardwired | Alt B: Battery Wireless (Türwächter) | Alt C: Mechanical-only |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Acquisition** | Tender electrical contractor; specify conduit routes; obtain electrical sign-off; procure panel capacity | Qualify hardware vendor; verify BMS gateway compatibility; negotiate volume per-door pricing | Simple purchase; no specialist required |
| **Preparation** | Cable runs, conduit, panel wiring, electrician commissioning (2–4 hr/door) | Surface-mount device; battery insert; gateway configure; BMS pair (0.5–1 hr/door) | Surface-mount only (0.25 hr/door) |
| **Usage** | Monitor fire panel faults; coordinate panel maintenance; track cable-fault tickets | Monitor wireless dashboard; receive real-time alerts; manage gateway status and coverage maps | Schedule manual inspection rounds; log visits in CMMS or paper |
| **Maintenance** | Cable continuity checks; panel card replacement; contracted electrician visits | Battery replacement (annually); signal health checks; firmware updates via gateway | Annual physical inspection visit per door; paper log archiving; no remote diagnostics |
| **Disposal** | Cable extraction (asbestos risk in pre-1980 buildings); panel decommissioning | Battery disposal (WEEE); device swap without wall modification | Device removal; no environmental obligations |

**Key structural insight:** Alt A creates the highest P1 burden at Preparation and Maintenance in retrofit FM contracts. Alt E (do nothing) carries zero P1 burden — the primary reason it persists despite compliance gaps. The Türwächter (Alt B) wins on Preparation and Usage burden vs. Alt A, but creates a unique P1 burden at Maintenance (battery management) that Alt A does not have.

### 3.2 Job Map — Ulwick 8 Steps

| # | Step | Statement for Product Job | Relevant to Product | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DEFINE | Define which door points require monitoring, alarm thresholds, and compliance reporting requirements per client building | false | Scoping occurs at FM contract level before device selection |
| 2 | LOCATE | Map and record all emergency exit door points and hardware specifications across client buildings | false | Asset mapping precedes device deployment |
| 3 | PREPARE | Install monitoring devices, configure gateways, integrate with client BMS/CMMS, and commission alarm triggers | **true** | Device installation and commissioning is the core Türwächter deployment task |
| 4 | CONFIRM | Verify all monitoring devices trigger alarms within specification and events reach the FM dashboard correctly | **true** | Commissioning verification directly tests device reliability at scale |
| 5 | EXECUTE | Detect unauthorized door activations in real time, trigger alarms, and log events to FM monitoring platform | **true** | Primary operational function of the device |
| 6 | MONITOR | Track real-time device health — battery levels, wireless signal integrity, alarm frequency per door point | **true** | IoT reporting capability is the primary Türwächter differentiator vs. mechanical alternatives |
| 7 | MODIFY | Respond to alarms, investigate false positives, adjust device configuration, dispatch battery replacement | **true** | FM operator response workflows driven by device-generated events |
| 8 | CONCLUDE | Generate compliance reports, close inspection records, archive event logs, deliver evidence certificates to client | **true** | Event log → compliance document chain is the primary FM business value of the IoT layer |

### 3.3 Error Statements — P1 + P2

*Executor = FM safety compliance engineer. All statements use Format 1 (Minimize likelihood of…) or Format 2 (Increase ability to…). ONE KPI per statement.*

| ID | Step | Error Type | Statement | Pyramid | Prod-Related | Alt | ImpCat | Imp | Sat | **Score** | Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N01 | PREPARE | EFFORT | Minimize installation labor-hours per door point required to deploy wireless exit monitoring devices on existing door hardware without conduit runs or frame modification | P1 | true | B | time | 8 | 7 | 9 | — |
| N02 | PREPARE | EFFORT | Minimize cost per door point of electrical conduit routing and cable installation required to connect hardwired exit alarm contacts to fire panels in occupied retrofit buildings | P1 | true | A | cost | 9 | 3 | **15** | UNDERSERVED |
| N03 | MAINTENANCE | DETECTION | Minimize the number of wireless door monitoring devices reaching critical battery depletion without generating a preventive maintenance alert in the FM CMMS — measured by undetected depletions per 100 devices per year | P1 | true | B | reliability | 9 | 4 | **14** | UNDERSERVED |
| N04 | CONFIRM | ACCURACY | Minimize the likelihood that exit monitoring devices pass initial commissioning tests but fail to trigger an alarm under actual emergency conditions due to misconfiguration — measured by post-commissioning alarm failure rate per 1,000 devices | P2 | false | — | safety | 10 | 4 | **16** | UNDERSERVED |
| N05 | CONFIRM | ACCURACY | Minimize the likelihood of deploying exit monitoring configurations that render escape routes non-compliant with EN 179 / EN 1125 single-motion release requirements — measured by non-compliant configurations per 100 installations | P2 | false | — | safety | 10 | 6 | 14 | — |
| N06 | EXECUTE | THROUGHPUT | Minimize elapsed time between an unauthorized emergency exit door activation and alert notification reaching the responsible FM monitoring operator — measured in minutes per event | P2 | false | — | time | 9 | 5 | 13 | — |
| N07 | EXECUTE | ROBUSTNESS | Minimize the likelihood that emergency exit alarm devices fail to trigger under zero-voltage conditions (mains outage or complete battery depletion) — measured by alarm failure rate under power-loss test | P2 | false | — | safety | 10 | 7 | 13 | — |
| N08 | EXECUTE | DETECTION | Minimize the number of emergency exit door openings that occur without generating a monitoring alert during a standard operating period — measured by undetected events per 100 door activations per month | P2 | false | — | safety | 10 | 4 | **16** | UNDERSERVED |
| N09 | MONITOR | CONSISTENCY | Minimize variability in event reporting latency across door monitoring devices within a single client building — measured by maximum deviation from median report time in seconds | P2 | false | — | reliability | 7 | 4 | 10 | — |
| N10 | MONITOR | DETECTION | Minimize the number of battery depletion events that reach critical failure level before triggering a preventive maintenance work order in the FM CMMS — measured by ratio of critical-depletion incidents to total depletions | P2 | false | — | reliability | 8 | 4 | 12 | — |
| N11 | MODIFY | RECOVERY | Minimize time to restore exit monitoring coverage at a door point following device fault, wireless disconnection, or battery depletion — measured in hours from failure detection to restored monitoring coverage | P2 | false | — | time | 8 | 5 | 11 | — |
| N12 | MODIFY | ACCURACY | Minimize the false positive alarm rate from exit monitoring devices — unauthorized activation alerts triggered by door vibration, accidental contact, or signal interference — measured by false positives per 100 logged alarm events | P2 | false | — | skill | 8 | 4 | 12 | — |
| N13 | CONCLUDE | EFFORT | Minimize FM labor-hours required to compile emergency exit door event logs into audit-ready fire safety inspection certificates — measured in hours per compliance report per building | P2 | false | — | time | 9 | 3 | **15** | UNDERSERVED |
| N14 | CONCLUDE | ACCURACY | Minimize the likelihood that emergency exit event logs contain gaps or discontinuities that cause fire safety compliance documentation to fail regulatory audit — measured by audit failure rate per 100 compliance submissions | P2 | false | — | safety | 10 | 4 | **16** | UNDERSERVED |
| N15 | CONCLUDE | THROUGHPUT | Increase the ability to generate real-time portfolio-level compliance dashboards showing monitoring health across all client exit door points with data latency under 24 hours | P2 | false | — | time | 8 | 3 | 13 | — |
| N16 | DEFINE | EFFORT | Minimize time required to generate a complete inventory of emergency exit door points requiring monitoring across a new multi-building client portfolio — measured in hours per 100 doors scoped | P2 | false | — | time | 7 | 5 | 9 | — |
| N17 | ACQUIRE | EFFORT | Minimize the total cost-per-door-per-year of emergency exit monitoring across portfolios of 50+ client buildings — measured in EUR per door per year including hardware, installation, and ongoing maintenance | P1 | false | cross | cost | 9 | 4 | **14** | UNDERSERVED |
| N18 | ACQUIRE | ACCURACY | Minimize the likelihood of deploying wireless exit monitoring devices that require proprietary gateways incompatible with the client building's existing BMS protocol — measured by BMS integration rework rate per 100 deployments | P1 | true | B | reliability | 8 | 4 | 12 | — |
| N19 | ACQUIRE | EFFORT | Minimize evaluation time for FM procurement teams to qualify wireless exit monitoring hardware for multi-client portfolio standardization — measured in days from vendor shortlist to approved FM specification | P1 | true | B | time | 7 | 5 | 9 | — |

### 3.4 P3–P5 Bridge Needs

| Level | Role | Retrieval Answer | Bridge Question Answer | Market Need | ImpCat | Imp | Sat | Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P3 Role** | FM compliance engineer | "A professional whose audit documentation is accepted by fire marshals without question — trusted by clients, never surprised by inspections" | Product must produce tamper-evident, timestamped event logs as primary regulatory evidence without manual assembly | N20: Maximize completeness and tamper-evidence of emergency exit event logs that FM compliance engineers can present as primary evidence during unannounced fire safety inspections — without manual data aggregation | time | 8 | 3 | 13 |
| **P4 Image** | FM contract manager [Overseer] | "Building owner sees FM manager as operationally proactive; fire marshal sees FM as compliant and audit-ready without prompting" | Product must generate client-presentable compliance dashboards without FM analyst post-processing | N21: Minimize manual post-processing effort required to convert exit monitoring event data into client-facing compliance reports demonstrating FM operational excellence — measured in hours per monthly client report | time | 7 | 4 | 10 |
| **P5 Emotional** | FM compliance technician | "Want: confidence that every door point is covered and no gaps exist. Avoid: discovering an unmonitored door during a fire marshal inspection" | Product must surface coverage gaps proactively before they become incidents or audit failures | N22: Minimize FM compliance technicians' exposure to undiscovered monitoring coverage gaps across distributed client sites that could result in unrecorded incidents prior to fire safety inspection [hypothesis] | safety | 7 | 4 | 10 |

---

sections.title: ODI Summary
sections.content:
### Underserved Needs (score ≥ 14, importance > 7, satisfaction ≤ 5)

| ID | Score | Need (abbreviated) | Step | Prod-Related | Türwächter addresses? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N04 | 16 | Commissioning-passing devices that fail under real alarm conditions | CONFIRM | false | Partial — mechanical alarm is power-independent, but IoT misconfiguration risk remains |
| N08 | 16 | Undetected door openings during operating period | EXECUTE | false | Direct — IoT layer provides real-time event detection vs. mechanical-only gap |
| N14 | 16 | Audit-failing gaps in event logs | CONCLUDE | false | Direct — continuous IoT logging vs. manual inspection gaps |
| N02 | 15 | Hardwired cable cost in occupied retrofit buildings | PREPARE | true (Alt A) | Direct — wireless retrofit vs. hardwired is the primary positioning axis |
| N13 | 15 | Manual compilation of compliance certificates | CONCLUDE | false | Direct — automated event log export replaces manual FM paperwork |
| N03 | 14 | Battery depletion without CMMS alert | MAINTENANCE | true (Alt B) | Partial — low-battery alert via IoT exists but field reliability unconfirmed |
| N05 | 14 | Non-compliant escape route configuration | CONFIRM | false | Addressed — EN 179/1125 certification pre-satisfies this |
| N17 | 14 | Total cost-per-door-per-year at portfolio scale | ACQUIRE | false | Contested — requires documented ROI case (per constraint C7) |

### Strategic Pattern

The strongest unmet needs cluster at **CONFIRM** (post-deployment reliability) and **CONCLUDE** (compliance evidence automation). Both are product-agnostic (not product_related=true) — representing market-inherent pain that any solution must address. The Türwächter's IoT event logging directly attacks N08/N13/N14/N15/N20. The gap: **N04** (commissioning failure detection) and **N12** (false positive rate) are not currently addressed by any alternative — they represent a product development and deployment-quality opportunity.

---

sections.title: Incumbent Anchors
sections.content:
| Technology | VN Anchor Unit | Level | Market Share | Trend |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hardwired electronic alarm systems (door contacts → fire panel) | Door & Exit Monitoring System | L5 | ~45% | Stable — high switching cost once installed |
| Battery-powered wireless alarm devices (Türwächter class) | Door Guardian Device | L4 | ~35% | Growing — driven by retrofit FM contract growth |
| Mechanical-only door alarms | Door Guardian Device | L4 | ~20% | Declining — compliance pressure reducing paper-log acceptance |

**Displacement target:** Mechanical-only (L4, 20%) is the direct share capture opportunity. Hardwired (L5, 45%) is the long-term displacement opportunity in net-new or renovation contracts.

---

rows.Value: L4 Door Guardian Device → L5 Door & Exit Monitoring → L6e Safety & Compliance Management
B2B / service NAICS — Phase 1 skips BOM (1.4) and Edges (1.5)
Operate and maintain a client's built environment to achieve continuous safety compliance, operational readiness, and regulatory conformance while minimising client-side labour
L7 — service ecosystem: FM provider coordinates sub-contractors, vendors, and regulators toward operational readiness of client buildings

rows.Description: Full multi-discipline bundled contract (hard + soft + compliance)
MEP, fire safety, structural maintenance only

rows.Circumstance: 20+ client sites across mixed building types; requires standardised devices deployable per-site and centralised multi-building dashboard
rows.Alternatives change?: Yes — single-site solutions don't aggregate; wired solutions don't scale cost-effectively

rows.Circumstance: Inspection-only contracts; client demands digital, audit-proof evidence chains with no paper archives or manual log assembly
rows.Alternatives change?: Yes — documentation capability becomes the competitive differentiator

rows.Circumstance: One large building (hospital campus, corporate HQ); deep BMS integration required; wired alternatives reduce integration friction
rows.Alternatives change?: Yes — proprietary gateway friction (C6) tips decision toward wired for BMS-heavy sites

rows.Circumstance: MEP and fire safety maintenance only; device must be low-maintenance and high-reliability across diverse door hardware types
rows.Alternatives change?: Yes — maintenance overhead per device is the primary total cost driver

rows.Value: B2B — FM operator is buyer; building owner is beneficiary

rows.Lens Source: L6e job_family: "Inspect, test, document, and certify compliance with fire safety, health, and environmental regulations across client sites"
rows.Lens: "What detection and documentation capabilities does an FM safety compliance manager need to deliver continuous, evidence-grade monitoring of emergency exit doors across client building portfolios?"

rows.Statement: Operate and maintain a client's built environment to achieve continuous safety compliance, operational readiness, and regulatory conformance while minimising client-side labour

**Help Door & Exit Monitoring System to detect unauthorized emergency exit door activations and produce audit-ready event logs for FM compliance workflows — without requiring wired infrastructure or on-site inspection rounds**

rows.Alternative: Hardwired electronic alarm systems (door contacts wired to fire panel)
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: Requires mains cabling and conduit in retrofit buildings; electrician sign-off; €150–300 labour/door on top of hardware; single cable-fault can drop a circuit; asbestos risk in pre-1980 buildings

rows.Alternative: **Battery-powered wireless alarm devices (Türwächter class, LoRaWAN door sensors)**
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: **Battery replacement cycles (1–3 yr); wireless range limits (~30–60 m); proprietary protocol friction (EasyWave ≠ BACnet/KNX); higher unit cost than mechanical**

rows.Alternative: Mechanical-only door alarms (no monitoring, no wireless)
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: No event logging; no remote alerting; compliance evidence requires manual inspection; declining installed base; no portfolio visibility

rows.Alternative: CCTV / video monitoring at exit points
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: No alarm-on-open; requires active human review; high infrastructure cost; GDPR data-retention complexity; no audit-grade timestamped event log

rows.Alternative: Do nothing / manual inspection rounds [Status Quo]
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: **Zero P1 burden** — FM inspection labor cost only (est. €6–12/door/visit quarterly); compliance gap risk accepted

rows.Scenario: New client onboarding — FM deploys exit monitoring across 50+ doors
rows.L4 Positions in Play: Door Guardian Device, Wireless Radio Transmitter, Gateway / BUS Interface

rows.Scenario: Annual fire safety audit — FM generates compliance evidence for fire marshal
rows.L4 Positions in Play: Door Guardian Device (event log), Fire Safety Inspection Platform

rows.Scenario: Unauthorized exit incident — FM retrieves historical event logs for client incident report
rows.L4 Positions in Play: Door Guardian Device (log), Gateway / BUS Interface

rows.Scenario: Battery maintenance cycle — FM schedules battery replacement across all monitored doors
rows.L4 Positions in Play: Battery Power Unit, CMMS work order (L5)

rows.Person: FM safety compliance engineer / compliance technician

rows.Alt A: Hardwired: Tender electrical contractor; specify conduit routes; obtain electrical sign-off; procure panel capacity
rows.Alt B: Battery Wireless (Türwächter): Qualify hardware vendor; verify BMS gateway compatibility; negotiate volume per-door pricing

rows.Alt A: Hardwired: Cable runs, conduit, panel wiring, electrician commissioning (2–4 hr/door)
rows.Alt B: Battery Wireless (Türwächter): Surface-mount device; battery insert; gateway configure; BMS pair (0.5–1 hr/door)

rows.Alt A: Hardwired: Monitor fire panel faults; coordinate panel maintenance; track cable-fault tickets
rows.Alt B: Battery Wireless (Türwächter): Monitor wireless dashboard; receive real-time alerts; manage gateway status and coverage maps
rows.Alt C: Mechanical-only: Schedule manual inspection rounds; log visits in CMMS or paper

rows.Alt A: Hardwired: Cable continuity checks; panel card replacement; contracted electrician visits
rows.Alt B: Battery Wireless (Türwächter): Battery replacement (annually); signal health checks; firmware updates via gateway
rows.Alt C: Mechanical-only: Annual physical inspection visit per door; paper log archiving; no remote diagnostics

rows.Alt A: Hardwired: Cable extraction (asbestos risk in pre-1980 buildings); panel decommissioning
rows.Alt B: Battery Wireless (Türwächter): Battery disposal (WEEE); device swap without wall modification

rows.Statement for Product Job: Define which door points require monitoring, alarm thresholds, and compliance reporting requirements per client building
rows.Rationale: Scoping occurs at FM contract level before device selection

rows.Statement for Product Job: Map and record all emergency exit door points and hardware specifications across client buildings
Install monitoring devices, configure gateways, integrate with client BMS/CMMS, and commission alarm triggers
rows.Rationale: Device installation and commissioning is the core Türwächter deployment task

rows.Statement for Product Job: Verify all monitoring devices trigger alarms within specification and events reach the FM dashboard correctly
rows.Rationale: Commissioning verification directly tests device reliability at scale

rows.Statement for Product Job: Detect unauthorized door activations in real time, trigger alarms, and log events to FM monitoring platform
rows.Rationale: Primary operational function of the device

rows.Statement for Product Job: Track real-time device health — battery levels, wireless signal integrity, alarm frequency per door point
rows.Rationale: IoT reporting capability is the primary Türwächter differentiator vs. mechanical alternatives

rows.Statement for Product Job: Respond to alarms, investigate false positives, adjust device configuration, dispatch battery replacement
rows.Rationale: FM operator response workflows driven by device-generated events

rows.Statement for Product Job: Generate compliance reports, close inspection records, archive event logs, deliver evidence certificates to client
rows.Rationale: Event log → compliance document chain is the primary FM business value of the IoT layer

rows.Statement: Minimize installation labor-hours per door point required to deploy wireless exit monitoring devices on existing door hardware without conduit runs or frame modification
Minimize cost per door point of electrical conduit routing and cable installation required to connect hardwired exit alarm contacts to fire panels in occupied retrofit buildings

Minimize the number of wireless door monitoring devices reaching critical battery depletion without generating a preventive maintenance alert in the FM CMMS — measured by undetected depletions per 100 devices per year

Minimize the likelihood that exit monitoring devices pass initial commissioning tests but fail to trigger an alarm under actual emergency conditions due to misconfiguration — measured by post-commissioning alarm failure rate per 1,000 devices

Minimize the likelihood of deploying exit monitoring configurations that render escape routes non-compliant with EN 179 / EN 1125 single-motion release requirements — measured by non-compliant configurations per 100 installations
Minimize elapsed time between an unauthorized emergency exit door activation and alert notification reaching the responsible FM monitoring operator — measured in minutes per event

Minimize the likelihood that emergency exit alarm devices fail to trigger under zero-voltage conditions (mains outage or complete battery depletion) — measured by alarm failure rate under power-loss test

Minimize the number of emergency exit door openings that occur without generating a monitoring alert during a standard operating period — measured by undetected events per 100 door activations per month
Minimize variability in event reporting latency across door monitoring devices within a single client building — measured by maximum deviation from median report time in seconds

Minimize the number of battery depletion events that reach critical failure level before triggering a preventive maintenance work order in the FM CMMS — measured by ratio of critical-depletion incidents to total depletions

Minimize time to restore exit monitoring coverage at a door point following device fault, wireless disconnection, or battery depletion — measured in hours from failure detection to restored monitoring coverage

Minimize the false positive alarm rate from exit monitoring devices — unauthorized activation alerts triggered by door vibration, accidental contact, or signal interference — measured by false positives per 100 logged alarm events
Minimize FM labor-hours required to compile emergency exit door event logs into audit-ready fire safety inspection certificates — measured in hours per compliance report per building

Minimize the likelihood that emergency exit event logs contain gaps or discontinuities that cause fire safety compliance documentation to fail regulatory audit — measured by audit failure rate per 100 compliance submissions
Increase the ability to generate real-time portfolio-level compliance dashboards showing monitoring health across all client exit door points with data latency under 24 hours
Minimize time required to generate a complete inventory of emergency exit door points requiring monitoring across a new multi-building client portfolio — measured in hours per 100 doors scoped

Minimize the total cost-per-door-per-year of emergency exit monitoring across portfolios of 50+ client buildings — measured in EUR per door per year including hardware, installation, and ongoing maintenance

Minimize the likelihood of deploying wireless exit monitoring devices that require proprietary gateways incompatible with the client building's existing BMS protocol — measured by BMS integration rework rate per 100 deployments

Minimize evaluation time for FM procurement teams to qualify wireless exit monitoring hardware for multi-client portfolio standardization — measured in days from vendor shortlist to approved FM specification

rows.Retrieval Answer: "A professional whose audit documentation is accepted by fire marshals without question — trusted by clients, never surprised by inspections"
rows.Bridge Question Answer: Product must produce tamper-evident, timestamped event logs as primary regulatory evidence without manual assembly
rows.Market Need: N20: Maximize completeness and tamper-evidence of emergency exit event logs that FM compliance engineers can present as primary evidence during unannounced fire safety inspections — without manual data aggregation

rows.Retrieval Answer: "Building owner sees FM manager as operationally proactive; fire marshal sees FM as compliant and audit-ready without prompting"
rows.Bridge Question Answer: Product must generate client-presentable compliance dashboards without FM analyst post-processing
rows.Market Need: N21: Minimize manual post-processing effort required to convert exit monitoring event data into client-facing compliance reports demonstrating FM operational excellence — measured in hours per monthly client report

rows.Retrieval Answer: "Want: confidence that every door point is covered and no gaps exist. Avoid: discovering an unmonitored door during a fire marshal inspection"
rows.Bridge Question Answer: Product must surface coverage gaps proactively before they become incidents or audit failures
rows.Market Need: N22: Minimize FM compliance technicians' exposure to undiscovered monitoring coverage gaps across distributed client sites that could result in unrecorded incidents prior to fire safety inspection [hypothesis]

rows.Need (abbreviated): Commissioning-passing devices that fail under real alarm conditions
rows.Türwächter addresses?: Partial — mechanical alarm is power-independent, but IoT misconfiguration risk remains

rows.Need (abbreviated): Undetected door openings during operating period
rows.Türwächter addresses?: Direct — IoT layer provides real-time event detection vs. mechanical-only gap
Direct — continuous IoT logging vs. manual inspection gaps

rows.Need (abbreviated): Hardwired cable cost in occupied retrofit buildings
rows.Türwächter addresses?: Direct — wireless retrofit vs. hardwired is the primary positioning axis
Direct — automated event log export replaces manual FM paperwork
Partial — low-battery alert via IoT exists but field reliability unconfirmed
Contested — requires documented ROI case (per constraint C7)

rows.Technology: Hardwired electronic alarm systems (door contacts → fire panel)
rows.Trend: Stable — high switching cost once installed
Growing — driven by retrofit FM contract growth
Declining — compliance pressure reducing paper-log acceptance

entities.label: JobMap
entities.product_job_statement: Help Door & Exit Monitoring System to detect unauthorized emergency exit door activations and produce audit-ready event logs for FM compliance workflows — without requiring wired infrastructure or on-site inspection rounds
entities.cfj_statement: Operate and maintain a client's built environment to achieve continuous safety compliance, operational readiness, and regulatory conformance while minimising client-side labour

entities.label: JobStep
entities.statement: Define which door points require monitoring, alarm thresholds, and compliance reporting requirements per client building
entities.relevance_rationale: Scoping occurs at FM contract level before device selection; product does not influence this step

entities.label: JobStep
entities.statement: Map and record all emergency exit door points and hardware specifications across client buildings
entities.relevance_rationale: Asset mapping precedes device deployment; product does not influence inventory creation

entities.label: JobStep
entities.statement: Install monitoring devices, configure gateways, integrate with client BMS/CMMS, and commission alarm triggers across all door points
entities.relevance_rationale: Device installation and commissioning is the core Türwächter deployment task; installation method (surface-mount wireless vs. wired) directly determines FM labor cost

entities.label: JobStep
entities.statement: Verify all monitoring devices trigger alarms within specification and that events reach the FM dashboard correctly before handover
entities.relevance_rationale: Post-commissioning verification tests device reliability; misconfiguration at this step is the leading cause of compliance failures discovered during fire marshal inspections

entities.label: JobStep
entities.statement: Detect unauthorized door activations in real time, trigger audible alarms, and log events timestamped to the FM monitoring platform
entities.relevance_rationale: Primary operational function of the device; detection latency and alarm reliability are the core performance KPIs

entities.label: JobStep
entities.statement: Track real-time device health — battery levels, wireless signal integrity, and alarm frequency per door point — across the FM portfolio
entities.relevance_rationale: IoT reporting capability (battery alerts, signal health, dashboard coverage maps) is the primary differentiator of the Türwächter vs. mechanical-only alternatives

entities.label: JobStep
entities.statement: Respond to alarms, investigate false positives, adjust device configuration, and dispatch maintenance work orders for battery replacement or fault resolution
entities.relevance_rationale: FM operator response workflows are triggered by device-generated events; false positive rate and recovery time directly affect FM labor costs

entities.label: JobStep
entities.statement: Generate compliance reports, close inspection records, archive event logs, and deliver fire safety evidence certificates to clients and regulators
entities.relevance_rationale: Automated event log → compliance document chain is the primary FM business value of the IoT layer; manual compilation is the top underserved need in this market

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize installation labor-hours per door point required to deploy wireless exit monitoring devices on existing door hardware without conduit runs or frame modification
entities.rationale: Wireless surface-mount deployment is the key install-time advantage vs. hardwired; already partially satisfied by the Türwächter's 0.5–1 hr/door profile

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize cost per door point of electrical conduit routing and cable installation required to connect hardwired exit alarm contacts to fire panels in occupied retrofit buildings
entities.rationale: Retrofit cable runs in occupied buildings cost €150–300 labour per door on top of hardware; this pain point is poorly addressed by all hardwired alternatives and is well-documented in FM procurement data

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize the number of wireless door monitoring devices reaching critical battery depletion without generating a preventive maintenance alert in the FM CMMS — measured by undetected depletions per 100 devices per year
entities.rationale: Battery life is not published by GfS for the IoT variant; FM operators report that undiscovered battery failures are a leading cause of monitoring coverage gaps in wireless safety hardware deployments

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood that exit monitoring devices pass initial commissioning tests but fail to trigger an alarm under actual emergency conditions due to misconfiguration — measured by post-commissioning alarm failure rate per 1,000 devices
entities.rationale: FM operators have no standardised post-commissioning verification protocol for door monitoring devices; commissioning failures are typically discovered during fire marshal inspections, not during deployment

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of deploying exit monitoring configurations that render escape routes non-compliant with EN 179 / EN 1125 single-motion release requirements — measured by non-compliant configurations per 100 installations
entities.rationale: Türwächter's EN 179/1125 certification pre-satisfies this; satisfaction is higher than N04 because the product is designed to the standard. Remains important for FM operators deploying third-party or mixed-vendor solutions.

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize elapsed time between an unauthorized emergency exit door activation and alert notification reaching the responsible FM monitoring operator — measured in minutes per event
entities.rationale: Alert latency is partially addressed by existing wireless systems but varies significantly across gateway configurations and building RF environments

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood that emergency exit alarm devices fail to trigger under zero-voltage conditions (mains outage or complete battery depletion) — measured by alarm failure rate under power-loss test
entities.rationale: Türwächter's mechanical-primary architecture satisfies this well (alarm works at 0V). Satisfaction=7 reflects the Türwächter's strength here. Hardwired alternatives also satisfy this via fire panel battery backup.

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize the number of emergency exit door openings that occur without generating a monitoring alert during a standard operating period — measured by undetected events per 100 door activations per month
entities.rationale: Mechanical-only and manual-inspection alternatives generate significant undetected openings. IoT monitoring directly addresses this but gaps exist during gateway outages, wireless coverage holes, and battery depletion periods.

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize variability in event reporting latency across door monitoring devices within a single client building — measured by maximum deviation from median report time in seconds
entities.rationale: Mixed-vendor or mixed-protocol deployments create inconsistent reporting cadences that complicate FM dashboard interpretation

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize the number of battery depletion events that reach critical failure level before triggering a preventive maintenance work order in the FM CMMS — measured by ratio of critical-depletion incidents to total depletions
entities.rationale: CMMS integration for battery health alerts is available in principle but rarely configured in practice; FM operators frequently discover depleted batteries reactively during manual rounds

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize time to restore exit monitoring coverage at a door point following device fault, wireless disconnection, or battery depletion — measured in hours from failure detection to restored monitoring coverage
entities.rationale: Recovery time depends on dispatch speed, spare parts availability, and CMMS work order routing — all external to the device itself

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize the false positive alarm rate from exit monitoring devices — unauthorized activation alerts triggered by door vibration, accidental contact, or signal interference — measured by false positives per 100 logged alarm events
entities.rationale: Pre-alarm feature on the Türwächter reduces false positives but does not eliminate them in high-traffic doors; FM operators report alarm fatigue in dense deployments as a barrier to dashboard adoption

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize FM labor-hours required to compile emergency exit door event logs into audit-ready fire safety inspection certificates — measured in hours per compliance report per building
entities.rationale: Manual compliance documentation is confirmed as a top FM pain point by industry sources (Facilitate Magazine 2026: fragmented record-keeping between contractors). IoT event logs that auto-generate compliance certificates directly address this.

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood that emergency exit event logs contain gaps or discontinuities that cause fire safety compliance documentation to fail regulatory audit — measured by audit failure rate per 100 compliance submissions
entities.rationale: Compliance documentation failures are the most severe commercial risk for FM operators — they expose the FM company to liability and client contract penalties. Continuous IoT logging eliminates gaps that manual inspection cannot prevent.

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Increase the ability to generate real-time portfolio-level compliance dashboards showing monitoring health across all client exit door points with data latency under 24 hours
entities.rationale: Portfolio FM operators (PFM segment) require cross-site visibility that is not available with mechanical or per-building hardwired systems; current IoT dashboards are per-gateway, not portfolio-aggregated

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize time required to generate a complete inventory of emergency exit door points requiring monitoring across a new multi-building client portfolio — measured in hours per 100 doors scoped
entities.rationale: Asset discovery is a FM onboarding bottleneck but is addressed partially by CAFM/IWMS platforms; the Türwächter does not directly address this step

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize the total cost-per-door-per-year of emergency exit monitoring across portfolios of 50+ client buildings — measured in EUR per door per year including hardware, installation, and ongoing maintenance
entities.rationale: FM procurement teams negotiate per-door unit economics at scale; the Türwächter IoT variant at ~€730/unit requires a documented ROI case vs. mechanical (~€350) and manual inspection (~€30–50/door/year labor) alternatives

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of deploying wireless exit monitoring devices that require proprietary gateways incompatible with the client building's existing BMS protocol — measured by BMS integration rework rate per 100 deployments
entities.rationale: EasyWave protocol incompatibility with BACnet/KNX (Constraint C6) directly manifests here; FM operators on standardised BMS platforms face gateway procurement and integration costs not anticipated at spec time

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize evaluation time for FM procurement teams to qualify wireless exit monitoring hardware for multi-client portfolio standardization — measured in days from vendor shortlist to approved FM specification
entities.rationale: FM procurement standardisation cycles are slow (3–6 months) but this is a sector-wide process issue not specific to any alternative

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Maximize completeness and tamper-evidence of emergency exit event logs that FM compliance engineers can present as primary evidence during unannounced fire safety inspections — without manual data aggregation
entities.rationale: P3 Role Identity: FM compliance engineer wants to be the professional whose documentation holds up in any audit without scrambling. IoT continuous logging with tamper-evident timestamps is the enabling product capability.

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize manual post-processing effort required to convert exit monitoring event data into client-facing compliance reports demonstrating FM operational excellence — measured in hours per monthly client report
entities.rationale: P4 Image Identity: FM contract manager needs client-presentable outputs that demonstrate operational excellence without manual analyst effort; current CAFM integrations still require custom report configuration

entities.label: ErrorStatement
entities.full_statement: Minimize FM compliance technicians' exposure to undiscovered monitoring coverage gaps across distributed client sites that could result in unrecorded incidents prior to fire safety inspection [hypothesis]
entities.rationale: P5 Emotional: FM technicians working in distributed portfolios report anxiety about undiscovered gaps (unconfirmed — marked hypothesis pending interview validation). Proactive coverage-gap alerting on the IoT dashboard would address this.

entities.label: IncumbentAnchor
entities.rationale: Dominant installed base in commercial FM contracts; high switching cost once cabled; serves BACnet/KNX BMS environments natively

entities.label: IncumbentAnchor
entities.rationale: Legacy installed base; compliance pressure from fire safety regulators reducing paper-log acceptance; direct displacement target for the Türwächter

entities.label: Alternative
entities.name: Hardwired electronic alarm systems
trade_offs: Requires mains cabling and conduit in retrofit buildings
€150–300 electrician labour per door point on top of hardware
Asbestos risk in pre-1980 buildings during cable routing

entities.label: Alternative
entities.name: Battery-powered wireless alarm devices (Türwächter class)
trade_offs: EasyWave protocol requires GfS-specific BUS gateway for BACnet/KNX BMS integration
Higher unit cost than mechanical variants

entities.label: Alternative
entities.name: Mechanical-only door alarms
trade_offs: No remote monitoring or event logging
Compliance evidence requires manual inspection rounds
No portfolio-level visibility for FM operators
Declining regulatory acceptance of paper-based compliance logs

entities.label: Alternative
entities.name: CCTV / video monitoring at exit points
trade_offs: No alarm-on-open; requires active human review
High infrastructure cost and GDPR data-retention obligations
No audit-grade timestamped event log for fire safety compliance
Does not prevent unauthorized use — only records it

entities.label: Alternative
entities.name: Do nothing / manual inspection rounds
trade_offs: Zero P1 product burden — FM inspection labour only (~€6–12/door/visit quarterly)
Paper logs increasingly rejected by fire marshals under digital compliance directives
entities.existential_note: Zero P1 burden makes this the default choice at lower-scrutiny FM contracts; only regulatory enforcement pressure drives displacement
